note:
在对唐纳德·特朗普(Donald Trump)煽动性或误导性帖子的处理方式进行了数月的辩论和分歧后，Facebook员工希望首席执行官马克·扎克伯格(Mark Zuckerberg)解释说，如果这位自由世界的领导人利用这个社交网络破坏2020年美国总统大选的结果，公司会怎么做。
Facebook的一名员工本周早些时候在该公司内部沟通平台Workplace的一个群组中写道：“我确实认为，我们正走向一个有问题的场景，Facebook将被用来以历史上从未有过的方式，积极破坏美国选举的合法性。”
在过去的一周里，这种情况一直是Facebook内部激烈讨论的话题，现在也是其领导人的首要问题。约2900名员工要求扎克伯格在周四上午11点举行的全公司会议上公开发表讲话。PT.。
扎克伯格意料之中的讲话正值内部对该公司处理错误信息的能力日益担忧之际，以及该公司为确保其平台不会在美国总统大选前被用来扰乱或误导而采取的预防措施。尽管Facebook表示，它已经投入了更多的资金和资源，以避免在2016年大选期间重蹈覆辙，但一些员工认为这还不够。特朗普总统已经花了几个月的时间质疑即将到来的2020年大选的合法性，散布关于邮寄选票的错误信息，并拒绝透露他是否接受在11月输给民主党候选人乔·拜登的可能性。
今年7月，特朗普告诉福克斯新闻(Fox News)，他不确定自己是否会向拜登让步，这让人怀疑，如果这位前副总统赢得选举，权力是否会和平过渡。我得看看。我不会简单地说“是”。我不会说不，总统说。
在Facebook的内部留言板上，在周四的全体会议之前，关于特朗普选举问题的讨论仍然是文明的。员工们就审查在任总统关于选举结果的潜在虚假陈述的好处展开了辩论，其中一人指出，“这将是一项真正令人不安的政策，适用于全球。”
美国不能让Facebook在谈到我们民主的完整性时采取观望态度，“希拉里·克林顿(Hillary Clinton)的前外交政策发言人、非营利性倡导组织Responsible Tech的联合创始人杰西·莱里奇(Jesse Lehrich)说。“除非他们主动勾勒出明确的政策和执行机制来保障选举，否则政纲将被武器化，以破坏它。
你在Facebook或其他科技公司工作吗？我们很高兴收到你的来信。联系Craig.silverman@buzzfeed.com，ryan.mac@buzzfeed.com，或通过我们的举报热线渠道之一。
虽然有迹象表明，在特朗普违反规定的情况下，脸书将挺身而出--比如周三，脸书删除了总统发布的一段视频，在视频中，特朗普声称儿童“几乎不受”新冠肺炎的影响--但也有其他人表示，该公司正在屈服于右翼的批评声音。在Workplace最近的另一篇帖子中，一名高级工程师收集的内部证据显示，Facebook正在优待知名的保守派账户，以帮助他们从内容中删除事实核查。
作为回应，该公司取消了他的职位，并限制内部访问他引用的信息。根据BuzzFeed新闻看到的内部帖子，这名工程师周三被解雇。
随着内部紧张局势的加剧和士气处于低谷，一个致力于错误信息政策的内部Workplace小组对该公司如何处理事实核查内容的担忧激增。
上周五，在另一次全体会议上，员工们问扎克伯格，右翼出版物布莱巴特新闻(Breitbart News)在分享了一段宣传未经证实的治疗方法的视频后，如何才能继续成为Facebook新闻的合作伙伴，并表示口罩对于抗击这种新型冠状病毒是不必要的。这段视频在被从布莱巴特的页面上删除之前，在6个小时内就获得了1400万的点击量，尽管其他账号仍在分享它。
扎克伯格回避了这个问题，但确实指出，如果布莱巴特在90天内因发布错误信息而受到两次罢工，它可能会被从公司的新闻标签中删除。(Facebook新闻合作伙伴，包括BuzzFeed News和华盛顿邮报(The Washington Post)等数十种出版物，可以获得补偿，并在社交网络上的一个特别新闻选项卡中获得安置。)。
扎克伯格说：“这当然是他们因为错误信息而遭受的一次打击，但在过去的90天里，他们没有其他的打击。”“因此，根据我们现有的政策(顺便说一句，我认为这些政策在这方面总体上是相当合理的)，取消这些政策是没有意义的。”
但Facebook自己的一些员工收集的证据显示，布莱巴特-以及其他右翼媒体和人物，包括Turning Point USA创始人查理·柯克(Charlie Kirk)、特朗普的支持者钻石和希尔克(Diamond And Silk)，以及保守的视频制作非营利性组织普拉格大学(Prager University)-受到了特殊对待，帮助它避免了与公司政策的冲突。他们认为这是对右翼出版商和页面的优待模式的一部分，其中许多人声称社交网络对保守派有偏见。
Facebook发言人利兹·布尔乔瓦在一份声明中说：“我们尊重第三方事实核查人员对一条内容的评级。”当事实检查员应用评级时，我们会应用标签和降级。但我们有责任为惯犯管理我们的内部系统。我们对多个虚假评级适用额外的系统范围处罚，包括去货币化和无法做广告，除非我们确定这些评级中的一个或多个不值得承担额外的后果。&#34；
7月22日，Facebook的一名员工向公司内部错误信息政策小组发布了一条消息，指出Facebook似乎代表该出版物行事的人已经澄清了针对布莱巴特的一些错误信息攻击。
这位员工写道：“布莱巴特升级标记为‘紧急：当天结束’的事件在同一天得到解决，所有针对布莱巴特页面和他们域名的错误信息罢工都在没有任何解释的情况下被清除。”
这名员工表示，该公司负责全球公共政策的副总裁乔尔·卡普兰(Joel Kaplan)表示，对查理·柯克(Charlie Kirk)在Instagram上发布的一篇帖子的部分错误评级被标记为“优先”升级。卡普兰曾在乔治·W·布什(George W.Bush)政府任职，并因公开支持布雷特·卡瓦诺(Brett Kavanaugh)有争议的最高法院提名而招致批评。
PolitiFact的执行董事亚伦·沙洛克曼(Aaron Sharockman)告诉BuzzFeed新闻，Facebook的一名联系人确实打电话讨论了柯克的帖子。
沙洛克曼说：“我们和他们通了电话，他们想知道这个帖子是如何与这个项目相一致的。”这只是一个微小的不准确，还是我们认为有潜在有害影响的东西？
PolitiFact没有改变对这篇帖子的评级。“我们坚持己见，”沙洛克曼说。
Facebook过去的员工，包括Facebook前全球选举广告诚信主管雅尔·艾森斯塔特(Yaël Eisenstat)，都对卡普兰对内容执法决定的影响力表示担忧。她之前告诉BuzzFeed新闻，卡普兰华盛顿政策团队的一名成员试图影响一个保守组织发布的广告的广告执行决定。
Facebook没有回应为什么卡普兰会亲自干预这样的事情的问题。
这些干预和其他干预似乎违反了Facebook的官方政策，该政策要求希望对事实核查评级提出异议的出版商与Facebook负责的事实核查合作伙伴联系。
另一名员工在该公司内部的“错误信息政策”讨论组中写道：“政策人员似乎一直在代表*完全*右翼出版商干预事实核查，以避免他们再次犯规。”
公开表示对右翼页面明显特殊对待的个人也面临后果。在一个案例中，Facebook的一名高级工程师收集了多个保守派人物从Facebook员工(包括政策团队中的员工)那里获得独特帮助的例子，以消除对其内容的事实核查。他7月份的帖子被删除了，因为这违反了公司的“尊重沟通政策”。
根据Workplace另一名员工的帖子，在这名工程师的职位被解除后，他引用的相关内部“任务”作为所谓的特殊待遇的例子，被设置为私人的，员工无法访问。
“就我个人而言，这让我对这家公司感到愤怒和羞愧，”这名员工写道，以支持他们的同事。
这位工程师于2016年加入公司，最近在Instagram上工作。他于周三离开了公司。BuzzFeed新闻看到的一名内部帖子上的一名员工说，他们得到了工程师的许可，可以说解雇“不是自愿的”。
在被解雇前的一篇内部帖子中，这位工程师表示，他“被告知，如果我的帖子被发现违反了尊重员工之外的其他政策，法律和人力资源部将与他联系。”
Facebook否认这名员工已经被解雇，但表示这是因为他们违反了公司的规定。
“我们有一种开放的文化，鼓励员工畅所欲言，”布尔乔瓦说。
他被解雇的消息导致Facebook的一些员工表示，他们现在害怕在内部讨论中批评公司。一人表示，他们正在删除旧帖子和评论，而另一人则表示，这“并不是第一次用尊重的工作场所指导方针来狙击一位著名的公司政策/道德批评者。”
另一名员工表示：“(他)是这家公司的良知，是我们做正确事情的不知疲倦的代言人。”
这名被解雇的员工拒绝置评，并要求不具名，因为担心引起反响。
2019年9月，Facebook创始人兼首席执行官马克·扎克伯格和Facebook全球公共政策副总裁乔尔·卡普兰在离开国会山与参议员约翰·科宁的会议后聊天。
这位工程师收集的内部证据与一名为Facebook的一家美国事实核查合作伙伴工作的记者的经验一致。他们告诉BuzzFeed新闻，保守的页面经常直接向公司抱怨。
“不按程序办事的出版商，似乎多是右翼的。他们没有向事实核查人员提出上诉，而是立即打电话给他们在Facebook的代表，“这位记者说。由于他们没有被授权公开发言，他拒绝透露姓名。“他们直接跳起来，说‘审查制度，第一修正案，自由。’”
他们补充说：“我认为Facebook有点害怕他们，因为特朗普政府。”
Facebook通常会将专门的合作伙伴经理指派到追随者较多或广告预算较大的页面。他们帮助客户最大限度地利用该平台。但在工程师的帖子中确定的案例中，合作伙伴代表似乎寻求对右翼出版商的优惠待遇。这导致了Facebook人员给事实核查合作伙伴打电话，以及在事实核查人员不知情或没有参与的情况下，错误信息罢工似乎已从内容中删除的情况。
Facebook的一名员工在7月22日的帖子中重申了这位工程师的发现，发现了多起来自右翼页面的事实核查投诉升级的情况，在某些情况下，投诉在同一天得到了有利于该账户的解决。
根据这篇帖子，乔尔·卡普兰(Joel Kaplan)在Instagram上对查理·柯克(Charlie Kirk)的一篇帖子进行了事实核查，要求“尽快/在下午12点之前”解决。ET“。这名员工声称，PragerU的合作伙伴经理是一项“为期两周的努力”的一部分，目的是防止该网站被授予惯犯身份，而这一称号将限制其覆盖范围和广告特权。
该代表以“合作伙伴敏感性”为例指出，PragerU投放了大量广告，并辩称相关内容符合观点，因此不受事实核查。
Facebook没有回答为什么合作伙伴经理会以广告量为理由不对一组页面采取行动。
沙洛克曼说，PolitiFact在Facebook的联系人从未要求他们改变评级。但Facebook的代表确实会主动讨论一篇帖子是否属于观点，是否超出了该计划的范围。
“我们进行了讨论，我们的合作伙伴问我们，‘你们为什么要对此进行事实核查？你为什么会得出这个结论呢？你认为这是否符合Facebook在事实核查方面的规章制度？他说。
布尔乔瓦说：我们有时会联系事实核查人员，以澄清我们的指导方针和该计划的范围。
马克·普罗沃斯特管理着多个不断进步的大型Facebook页面，包括其他98%的页面，这是该平台上最大的页面之一。他说，他的Facebook合作伙伴经理的反应远远低于Facebook员工为右边的Provost同行记录的内容。他也不知道Facebook曾代表他联系过任何事实核查人员。
普罗沃斯特在谈到脸书时说：“我们10天内都不会收到回信。”“我想右翼得到了一笔更好的交易。”
他的另一个98%的页面目前有被删除的风险，因为它有三次罢工。教务长说，其中一次罢工是因为三个月前违反了仇恨言论，尽管到目前为止，Facebook还没有人告诉他这篇冒犯的帖子是什么。据教务长称，他的合作伙伴代表表示，他们将对此进行调查。那是上周的事了，他说他还没有收到最新消息。
“我对此感到非常沮丧，”教务长说。“最好的解决方案是，如果Facebook像他们指派的事实核查公司一样做出反应。”
普罗沃斯特说，当他联系Facebook的事实核查伙伴对评级提出异议时，很容易与他们打交道。在某些情况下，他提供了额外的证据，证明他声称自己改变了评级；在另一些情况下，他纠正了违规帖子，错误的评级被删除。
接受BuzzFeed新闻采访的事实核查人员表示，大多数页面所有者都遵循这项政策，并与他们联系，对评级提出异议。但在某些情况下，他们会直接听取被指派与事实核查人员合作的Facebook合作伙伴经理的意见。
“他们会问我们，‘你能再看看这个吗？你确定吗？‘“。收银员说。
在其他情况下，Facebook本身将悄悄地删除其合作伙伴之一应用的事实核查。这似乎就是3月25日钻石与丝绸(Diamond And Silk)的一篇帖子所发生的事情。两人在Facebook上写道：“该死的，为了给众议院议员加薪，怎么会拨出2500万美元，一点也不关心美国人，这将有助于刺激美国的经济？”告诉我怎么做？#PutamericansBackToWorkNow。“
Facebook的事实核查合作伙伴Lead Stories将这篇帖子评为虚假。戴蒙德和西尔克最初遵循了适当的程序，直接呼吁进行头条新闻。
《头条》主编艾伦·杜克(Alan Duke)告诉BuzzFeed新闻：“他们的呼吁很详细，我们很快就做出了回应。”“结果是，我们决定，最初给出内容的虚假评级应该向下修正为‘部分虚假’。”
但这一部分虚假的评级显然并不能让戴蒙德和西尔克满意，据报道，他们在传播冠状病毒错误信息后失去了在线福克斯新闻节目。正如7月22日Facebook内部帖子所述，戴蒙德和西尔克呼吁他们的合作伙伴经理，后者为这一问题开了一张内部罚单。Facebook的代表辩称，这篇帖子只是观点，并警告说，这两人“非常敏感，毫不犹豫地公开了他们对Facebook上所谓的保守派偏见的担忧。”
这位Facebook员工说，“有人在任务中被描述为‘政策/领导层’，他呼吁不仅要完全取消这次罢工，还要取消从1月份开始的罢工。”
note_en:
After months of debate and disagreement over the handling of inflammatory or misleading posts from Donald Trump, Facebook employees want CEO Mark Zuckerberg to explain what the company would do if the leader of the free world uses the social network to undermine the results of the 2020 US presidential election.
 “I do think we’re headed for a problematic scenario where Facebook is going to be used to aggressively undermine the legitimacy of the US elections, in a way that has never been possible in history,” one Facebook employee wrote in a group on Workplace, the company’s internal communication platform, earlier this week.
 For the past week, this scenario has been a topic of heated discussion inside Facebook and is now a top question for its leader. Some 2,900 employees asked that Zuckerberg address it publicly during a company-wide meeting on Thursday which is slated for 11 a.m. PT.
 Zuckerberg’s expected remarks come amid  growing internal concerns about the company's competence in handling misinformation, and the precautions it is taking to ensure its platform isn’t used to disrupt or mislead ahead of the US presidential election. Though Facebook says it has committed more money and resources to avoid repeating its failures during the 2016 election, some employees believe it isn’t enough. President Trump has already spent months raising questions about the legitimacy of the upcoming 2020 election,  spreading misinformation about mail-in ballots, and declining to say if he’d accept the possibility of losing to Democratic nominee Joe Biden in November.
 In July,  Trump told Fox News he wasn’t sure if he’d concede to Biden, casting doubt on whether there would be a peaceful transition of power if the former vice president wins the election. "I have to see. I'm not just going to say yes. I'm not going to say no," the president said.
    On Facebook’s internal message boards, discussion about the Trump election question remained civil prior to Thursday’s all-hands meeting. Employees debated the merits of censoring a sitting president’s potentially false statements about election results with one person noting that “it would be a really troubling policy to apply globally.”
 "America can't afford for Facebook to take a wait-and-see approach when it comes to the integrity of our democracy,” said Jesse Lehrich, a former foreign policy spokesperson to Hillary Clinton and cofounder of Accountable Tech, a nonprofit advocacy group. “Unless they proactively outline clear policies and enforcement mechanisms to safeguard the election, the platform  will be weaponized to undermine it."
 Do you work at Facebook or another technology company? We'd love to hear from you. Reach out at  craig.silverman@buzzfeed.com,   ryan.mac@buzzfeed.com,  or  via one of our   tip line channels .
 While there are signs Facebook will stand up to Trump in cases where he violates its rules — as on Wednesday when  it removed a video post from the president in which he claimed that children are “almost immune” to  COVID-19 — there are others who suggest the company is caving to critical voices on the right. In another recent Workplace post, a senior engineer collected internal evidence that showed Facebook was giving preferential treatment to prominent conservative accounts to help them remove fact-checks from their content.
 The company responded by removing his post and restricting internal access to the information he cited. On Wednesday the engineer was fired, according to internal posts seen by BuzzFeed News.
  With  heightened internal tensions and morale at a low point, concerns about how the company handles fact-checked content have exploded in an internal Workplace group dedicated to misinformation policy.
 Last Friday, at another all-hands meeting, employees asked Zuckerberg how right-wing publication Breitbart News could remain a Facebook News partner after sharing a video that promoted unproven treatments and said masks were unnecessary to combat the novel coronavirus. The video racked up 14 million views in six hours  before it was removed from Breitbart’s page, though other accounts continued to share it.
 Zuckerberg danced around the question but did note that Breitbart could be removed from the company's news tab if it were to receive two strikes for publishing misinformation within 90 days of each other. (Facebook News partners, which include dozens of publications such as BuzzFeed News and the Washington Post, receive compensation and placement in a special news tab on the social network.)
 “This was certainly one strike against them for misinformation, but they don't have others in the last 90 days,” Zuckerberg said. “So by the policies that we have, which by the way I think are generally pretty reasonable on this, it doesn't make sense to remove them.”
 But some of Facebook’s own employees gathered evidence they say shows Breitbart — along with other right-wing outlets and figures including Turning Point USA founder Charlie Kirk, Trump supporters Diamond and Silk, and conservative video production nonprofit  Prager University — has received special treatment that helped it avoid running afoul of company policy. They see it as part of a pattern of preferential treatment for right-wing publishers and pages, many of which have alleged that the social network is biased against conservatives.
 “We defer to third-party fact-checkers on the rating that a piece of content receives," Facebook spokesperson Liz Bourgeois said in a statement. "When a fact checker applies a rating, we apply a label and demotion. But we are responsible for how we manage our internal systems for repeat offenders. We apply additional system wide penalties for multiple false ratings, including demonetization and the inability to advertise, unless we determine that one or more of those ratings does not warrant additional consequences."
 On July 22, a Facebook employee posted a message to the company’s internal misinformation policy group noting that some misinformation strikes against Breitbart had been cleared by someone at Facebook seemingly acting on the publication's behalf.
 “A Breitbart escalation marked ‘urgent: end of day’ was resolved on the same day, with all misinformation strikes against Breitbart’s page and against their domain cleared without explanation,” the employee wrote.
 The same employee said a partly false rating applied to an Instagram post from Charlie Kirk was flagged for “priority” escalation by Joel Kaplan, the company’s vice president of global public policy. Kaplan once served in George W. Bush’s administration and drew criticism for publicly supporting Brett Kavanaugh’s controversial nomination to the Supreme Court.
 Aaron Sharockman, the executive director of PolitiFact, told BuzzFeed News a contact at Facebook did call to discuss Kirk’s post.
 “We had a call with them where they wanted to know how this post was aligned with the program,” Sharockman said. "Was this just a minor inaccuracy or was it something that we thought was something that had potential harmful effects?"
 PolitiFact did not change its rating on the post. “We stuck to our guns there,” Sharockman said.
 Past Facebook employees, including Yaël Eisenstat, Facebook's former global election ads integrity lead, have expressed concerns with Kaplan’s influence over content enforcement decisions. She  previously told BuzzFeed News a member of Kaplan's Washington policy team attempted to influence ad enforcement decisions for an ad placed by a conservative organization.
 Facebook did not respond to questions about why Kaplan would personally intervene in matters like this.
    These and other interventions appear to be in violation of Facebook’s  official policy, which requires publishers wishing to dispute a fact check rating to contact the Facebook fact-checking partner responsible.
 “It appears that policy people have been intervening in fact-checks on behalf of *exclusively* right-wing publishers, to avoid them getting repeat-offender status,” wrote another employee in the company’s internal “misinformation policy” discussion group.
 Individuals that spoke out about the apparent special treatment of right-wing pages have also faced consequences. In one case, a senior Facebook engineer collected multiple instances of conservative figures receiving unique help from Facebook employees, including those on the policy team, to remove fact-checks on their content. His July post was removed because it violated the company’s “respectful communication policy.”
 After the engineer’s post was removed, the related internal “tasks” he’d cited as examples of the alleged special treatment were made private and inaccessible to employees, according to a Workplace post from another employee.
 “Personally this makes me so angry and ashamed of this company,” wrote the employee in support of their colleague.
 The engineer joined the company in 2016 and most recently worked on Instagram. He left the company on Wednesday. One employee on an internal thread seen by BuzzFeed News said that they received permission from the engineer to say that the dismissal “was not voluntary.”
 In an internal post before his dismissal, the engineer said he was “told to expect to be contacted by legal and HR if my post is found to be violating other policies in addition to Respectful Comms.”
 Facebook denied the employee had been terminated for the post but said it was because "they broke the company's rules."
 “We have an open culture and encourage employees to speak out about concerns they have," Bourgeois said.
 News of his firing caused some Facebook employees to say that they now fear speaking critically about the company in internal discussions. One person said they were deleting old posts and comments, while another said this was “hardly the first time the respectful workplace guidelines have been used to snipe a prominent critic of company policies/ethics.”
 “[He] was a conscience of this company, and a tireless voice for us doing the right thing,” said another employee.
 The terminated employee declined to comment and asked not to be named for fear of repercussions.
  Facebook founder and CEO Mark Zuckerberg and Facebook's Vice President of Global Public Policy Joel Kaplan chat after leaving a meeting with Sen. John Cornyn on Capitol Hill in September 2019.
  The internal evidence gathered by the engineer aligns with the experience of a journalist who works for one of Facebook’s US fact-checking partners. They told BuzzFeed News that conservative pages often complain directly to the company.
 “Of the publishers that don’t follow the procedure, it seems to be mostly ones on the right. Instead of appealing to the fact-checker they immediately call their rep at Facebook,” said the journalist, who declined to be named because they were not authorized to speak publicly. “They jump straight up and say ‘censorship, First Amendment, freedom.’”
 “I think Facebook is a bit afraid of them because of the Trump administration,” they added.
    Facebook typically assigns dedicated partner managers to pages with large followings or big ad budgets. They help their clients maximize their use of the platform. But in the cases identified in the engineer's post, partner reps appear to have sought preferential treatment for right-wing publishers. This resulted in phone calls to fact-checking partners from people at Facebook, and instances where misinformation strikes appear to have been removed from content without a fact-checker’s knowledge or involvement.
 A Facebook employee’s July 22 post restating the engineer’s findings identified multiple cases in which a fact-check complaint from a right-wing page was escalated and in some cases resolved in the account’s favor the same day.
 According to the post, Joel Kaplan flagged a fact check of a Charlie Kirk Instagram post for resolution “ASAP/before 12 p.m. ET”. This same employee claimed PragerU's partner manager was part of a “two weeks long effort” to prevent the site from being given Repeat Offender status, a designation that would have limited its reach and advertising privileges.
 Citing “partner sensitivity," the rep noted that PragerU runs a lot of ads, and argued that the content in question qualified as opinion and was therefore exempt from being fact-checked.
 Facebook did not answer questions about why a partner manager would cite ad volume as a reason for not acting against a group of pages.
 Sharockman said PolitiFact’s contacts at Facebook have never asked them to change a rating. But Facebook reps do reach out to discuss whether a post is opinion or otherwise outside of the scope of the program.
 “We have had discussions where our partners have asked us, ‘Why did you fact check this? Why did you come to this conclusion? How do you think it fits within the scope of Facebook's rules or regulations within fact-checking?’” he said.
 "We sometimes reach out to fact-checkers to clarify our guidelines and scope of the program," Bourgeois said.
 Mark Provost manages multiple large progressive Facebook pages, including The Other 98%, one of the biggest on the platform. He said his Facebook partner manager is far less responsive than what the Facebook employee documented for Provost’s counterparts on the right. And he’s not aware of any case where Facebook contacted a fact-checker on his behalf.
 “We don't get a message back for 10 days,” Provost said of Facebook. “I imagine the right wing is getting a way better deal.”
 His Other 98% page is currently at risk of deletion because it has three strikes. Provost said one strike is for a hate speech violation three months ago, though as of now no one at Facebook has told him what the offending post was. His partner representative said they would look into it, according to Provost. That was last week and he says he still hasn’t received an update.
 “I'm getting so frustrated with this,” Provost said. “The best solution would be if Facebook were as responsive as the fact-checking companies that they've assigned.”
 Provost said Facebook’s fact-checking partners are easy to deal with when he contacts them to dispute a rating. In some cases, he provided additional proof of his claim to get a rating changed; in other cases, he corrected the offending post and the false rating was removed.
 The fact-checker who spoke to BuzzFeed News said most page owners follow the policy and contact them to dispute a rating. But in some cases, they hear directly from the Facebook partner manager assigned to work with fact-checkers.
 “They will ask us, ‘Could you take a look at this again? Are you sure?’” the checker said.
 In other cases, Facebook itself will quietly remove a fact-check applied by one of its partners. That appears to be what happened with a March 25 post from Diamond and Silk. The duo  wrote on Facebook, “How the hell is allocating 25 million dollars in order to give a raise to house members, that don't give a damn about Americans, going to help stimulate America's economy? Tell me how? #PutAmericansBackToWorkNow.”
 Lead Stories, a Facebook fact-checking partner, rated the post false. Diamond and Silk initially followed the proper procedure and appealed directly to Lead Stories.
 “They were detailed in their appeal and we replied promptly,” Alan Duke, the editor-in-chief of Lead Stories, told BuzzFeed News. “The result was that we decided that a false rating initially given their content should be revised downward to ‘partly false.’”
 But that partly false rating apparently didn’t satisfy Diamond and Silk, who  reportedly lost their online Fox News show after spreading coronavirus misinformation. As detailed in the July 22 internal Facebook post, Diamond and Silk appealed to their partner manager, who opened an internal ticket for the issue. The Facebook rep argued the post was opinion and warned that the duo “is extremely sensitive and has not hesitated going public about their concerns around alleged conservative bias on Facebook.”
 The Facebook employee said that “someone described on the task as ‘Policy/Leadership’ made the call to not only completely remove this strike, but also the one from January.” ●
title: 工作人员询问扎克伯格，如果特朗普利用Facebook对选举提出异议，会发生什么；一些工作人员表示，他们有证据表明他们偏袒右翼页面
title_en: Staffers ask Zuckerberg what would happen if Trump uses Facebook to dispute the election; some staff say they have evidence of favoritism for right-wing pages

note:
受Twitter上一场有趣的讨论的启发，我最近花了很多时间思考不同的工作证明机制。
当我说工作证明时，我说的不是一些加密货币使用的共识算法。我说的是社交网络。
就其核心而言，社交网络主要是关于一件事：通过信号建立社交资本。正如我在信令即服务中所写的，信令可以分为三个不同的组件：
例如：巴塔哥尼亚背心既象征着亲社会的态度(“我关心环境”)，也象征着财富(“我买得起500美元的夹克”)。根据你住在哪里，这也可能是关于你职业的信号。
为了将这些信息传递给他人并建立实际的社会资本，您需要一个信号分发渠道。一种选择是在公共场合穿着背心，其他人可以看到它-但你能够接触到的观众规模有明显的物理限制。
他们的主要角色是大规模分发信号信息，并将其转化为可量化的社会资本(以点赞和追随者的形式)。
随着社交网络的发展，它们增加了你的信号信息的潜在覆盖面-但它们也挤满了地位竞争对手。这就是为什么社交网络通常会为你提供一套信号放大工具。这些工具可以帮助您提升您的信令信息，并脱颖而出。
在“信号即服务”一书中，我将信号放大与尤金·魏(Eugene魏)的工作证明障碍进行了比较，他对此的描述如下：
几乎每个值得注意的社交网络都有一个早期签名的工作障碍证明。对于Facebook，它发布了一些诙谐的基于文本的状态更新。在Instagram上，它发布了一张有趣的广场照片。对于Vine来说，这是一段有趣的6秒视频。对于Twitter来说，它是在写一段140个字符或更少的有趣的文本。Pinterest？钉上一张引人注目的照片。您可能会得出其他网络的工作证明，如Quora、Reddit和Twitch等等。成功的社交网络不会一开始就提出欺骗性的问题，他们想从你那里得到什么通常是很清楚的。
但是我想得越多，我就越不喜欢这种比较。实际上，我认为尤金的工作证明理论只触及了社交网络实际作用的皮毛。
看看这张非常陈词滥调的Instagram照片。摄影师显然在构图上花了很多心思和努力，并应用了不同的滤镜和编辑工具来使它看起来更好看。
这是尤金对工作证明定义的一个完美例子。更准确地说，是创造性工作的证明。
编辑你的照片有助于放大你的信号信息，让你在Instagram的状态舞台(也就是新闻馈送)中脱颖而出。它还会在你的帖子中添加额外的信号信息：“看看我是多么伟大的摄影师”或“我是一个有创造力的人”。
但这些并不是您在这里传达的主要信令信息。通过这张照片，你真正想告诉你的追随者的是“我是一个环游世界的旅行者”和“我正处于一段幸福的关系中”(反过来，这也是财富和交配价值的代言人)。
因此，社交网络不仅是信令分发(和放大)网络-它们还允许用户证明他们的信令消息。
创造性的工作证明只是借口，有助于提升你的帖子。更重要的是社交网络提供的额外证据机制。就Instagram而言，这些是照片和位置标签。
当新的社交网络出现时，他们必须引入新的证明机制，将自己与现有的现有公司区分开来。这些既可以是新颖的创造性工作证明障碍，也可以是全新的证明x机制。
TikTok是创造性工作创新的一个很好的例子。这款应用程序为创作者提供了一套强大的视频编辑工具，开启了一个全新的创意水平。
参与TikTok状态游戏的成本比Instagram高得多(将TikTok上制作精良的舞蹈编排与你在Instagram上发布的中值旅游帖子进行比较)-但其强大的Feed算法也让发现变得更容易，从而更快地回报用户，从而获得更多的社交资本。
不过，TikTok除了其新颖的创造性工作障碍之外，并没有增加任何新的证据。你可以发出信号，证明你的创造力，但你也可以通过将视频上传到Instagram来实现同样的目的。
另一方面，斯特拉瓦引入了一种全新的证明机制：身体活动证明。通过使用手机的GPS传感器(或第三方健身跟踪器)，用户实际上可以证明他们跑步或骑自行车的距离和速度。与Instagram照片相比，斯特拉瓦的证明机制更难伪造(尽管也有例外)。
Strava的伟大之处在于，它强化了一种实际上对你有好处的行为：虽然最初让你进入这款应用的状态游戏可能是零和游戏，但你必须进行的实际体育锻炼会产生非常积极的、复合的效果。
问题是：我们还应该建立什么样的社交网络，才能有类似的正反馈循环呢？他们的证明机制是什么？
我喜欢斯特拉瓦烹饪的想法，但我非常怀疑它是否能被建造出来。为什么？因为必要的证据机制并不存在。
烹饪时你优化的主要指标是味道。但你将如何衡量或量化口味呢？我们拥有的最接近味道的指标是光学：你做的饭看起来有多好吃？这一点很容易用一张照片来证明。但这是Instagram已经提供的工作证明机制(包括让你的食物看起来更美味的过滤器)。只要没有人拿出更好的烹饪证明机制，我认为我们就不太可能在这个领域看到一个成功的社交网络。
虽然学习本身的活动可能很难量化，但你可以衡量学习的结果：知识。有没有人造过多人版的安琪？闪存卡将是一种完美的知识证明机制，可以很容易地变成一种与朋友竞争的游戏。
与Strava例子中的身体活动类似，学习并不是大多数人喜欢做的事情。正如TikTok创始人亚历克斯·朱指出的那样，教育有点违背人性。但是，结合足够强大的信号机制，您可以让用户参与进来。这与克里斯·迪克森(Chris Dixon)著名的“为工具而来，为网络而留”的战略有点相反。为状态而来，为工具而来。
我很想看到的一个相关产品是Strava for Reding。想象一下，一个电子书阅读器不仅可以记录你花在阅读上的时间，还可以记录你正在阅读的内容。基于这些(阅读)验证机制，您可以构建Streak或GitHub之类的可视化效果，以激励用户阅读更多(更有规律)。
您甚至可以根据您阅读的书籍和文章的内容，为不同的主题构建排行榜。或者考虑一个分数，表明你每个话题的阅读行为是如何平衡的(以激励用户从不同的角度阅读政治话题的摘要)。
不幸的是，我认为我们不太可能在短期内看到我所描述的产品。世界上最大的书店、最受欢迎的电子书阅读器和最大的图书社交网络都属于一家在设计和面向用户的产品创新方面几乎没有能力的公司。
(附注：亚马逊对图书的垄断可能是科技领域最被低估的次优均衡。)。
另一款有趣的应用是社交投资应用。想想“罗宾汉身份，而是一个社交网络”。投资似乎已经是一种相当不错的社会活动--只要看看r/wallstreetbets这样的社区就知道了。正如帕特罗指出的那样，“罗宾汉”给人的感觉更像是一款游戏，而不是一款金融应用。
那么，为什么不建立一个投资应用程序，打开它，显示你所有朋友的投资和他们随着时间的推移的回报呢？不用在Reddit和Instagram上分享截图，你可以在这款应用程序中证明你的投资是正确的。
请注意，像这样的应用程序不会是关于财富的信号。这是关于发出信号是正确的，并有能力证明这一点。这可能是一个比发出财富信号更强大、更吸引人的机制--这也是我仍然看好预测市场的原因。
也许一个设计良好、对消费者友好的预测市场应用程序将是最终证明x的社交网络。斯特拉瓦是对的。
当我们谈到“正确”这个话题时：你同意我在这篇文章中的想法吗？您还想看到哪些社交网络和x证明机制？
感谢Dan Romero，Des Traynor，Jan König，Max Cutler和Zack Hargett阅读这篇文章的草稿。
note_en:
Sparked by  an interesting Twitter discussion, I’ve spent a lot of time recently thinking about different proof-of-work mechanisms.
 When I say proof-of-work, I’m not talking about consensus algorithms like the ones that some crypto currencies use. I’m talking about social networks.
 At their core, social networks are primarily about one thing: Building social capital through signaling. As I wrote in  Signaling as a Service, signaling can be broken down into three different components:
  For example: A Patagonia vest signals both a prosocial attitude ( “I care about the environment“) as well as wealth (“ I can afford to spend $500 on a jacket“). Depending on where you live, it might also signal something about  your occupation.
  In order to signal these messages to others and build actual social capital you need a signaling distribution channel. One option would be to wear the vest in public where others can see it – but there are obvious physical constraints to the size of the audience you’d be able to reach.
  Their primary role is to distribute signaling messages at scale and transform them into quantifiable social capital (in the form of likes and followers).
 As social networks grow, they increase the potential reach of your signaling messages – but they also get crowded with status rivals. This is why social networks typically provide you with a set of signaling amplification tools. These tools help you boost your signaling messages and stand out from the crowd.
 In  Signaling as a Service I compared signaling amplification to  Eugene Wei’s idea of proof-of-work hurdles, which he describes as follows:
 Almost every social network of note had an early signature proof of work hurdle. For Facebook it was posting some witty text-based status update. For Instagram, it was posting an interesting square photo. For Vine, an entertaining 6-second video. For Twitter, it was writing an amusing bit of text of 140 characters or fewer. Pinterest? Pinning a compelling photo. You can likely derive the proof of work for other networks like Quora and Reddit and Twitch and so on. Successful social networks don’t pose trick questions at the start, it’s usually clear what they want from you.
 But the more I think about it, the less I like the comparison. I actually think that Eugene’s proof-of-work theory only scratches the surface of what social networks actually do.
   Take a look at this very cliché Instagram picture. The photographer clearly put a lot of thought and effort into its composition and applied different filters and editing tools to make it look nicer.
  It’s a perfect example of Eugene’s definition of proof-of-work.  Proof-of-  creative-work, to be more exact.
 Editing your photo helps to amplify your signaling message and sets you apart within Instagram’s status arena (aka the newsfeed). It also adds additional signaling messages to your post:  “Look how great a photographer I am” or  “I’m a creative person”.
 But those are not the main signaling messages you are communicating here. What you really want to tell your followers with this photo is something along the lines of  “I’m a world-traveler” and  “I’m in a happy relationship”  (which in turn are also just signaling proxies for wealth and mating worthiness).
   Social networks are therefore not only signaling distribution (and amplification) networks – they also allow users to prove their signaling messages.
 The creative proof-of-work is just pretext and helps to boost your post. What’s more important are the additional proof mechanisms that social networks provide. In the case of Instagram those are photos and location tags.
   When new social networks emerge they have to introduce new proof mechanisms to differentiate themselves from existing incumbents. These can either be novel proof-of-creative-work hurdles or completely new proof-of-x mechanisms.
 TikTok is a good example for proof-of- creative-work innovation. The app provides creators with a powerful set of video editing tools that have opened a whole new level of creativity.
 The cost to participate in TikTok’s status game is a lot higher than Instagram’s  (compare a well-made dance choreography on TikTok to your median Instagram travel post) – but its powerful feed algorithms also make discovery easier and thus reward users faster and with more social capital.
 TikTok doesn’t add any new proof points beyond its novel creative work hurdle though. You can signal and prove your creativity but you could achieve the same by uploading your video to Instagram.
 Strava, on the other hand, introduced an entirely new proof mechanism: Proof-of-physical-activity. By using your phone’s GPS sensor (or a 3rd-party fitness tracker), users can actually prove how much and fast they ran or cycled. In contrast to  Instagram  photos, Strava’s proof mechanism is a lot harder to fake  (though there are  exceptions).
  What’s great about Strava is that it reinforces a behavior that’s actually good for you: While the status game that initially got you into the app might be zero sum, the actual physical exercise you have to put in to compete has a very positive, compounding effect.
 The question is: What other social networks should we build that could have similar positive feedback loops? And what are their proof mechanisms?
    I love the idea of a  Strava for Cooking – but I’m very skeptical that it can be build. Why? Because the necessary proof mechanisms don’t exist.
 The primary metric you optimize on when cooking is taste. But how would you measure or quantify taste? The closest proxy to taste that we have is optics: How good does the meal that you cooked look? This can easily be proved with a photo .. but that’s a proof-of-work mechanism that Instagram already offers (including filters to make your food look nicer). As long as no one comes up with a better proof mechanism for cooking, I think it’s unlikely that we will see a successful social network in the space.
  While the activity of learning itself might be hard to quantify, you can measure the outcome of learning: knowledge. Has anyone built a multiplayer version of  Anki yet? Flash cards would be a perfect proof-of-knowledge mechanism and could easily be turned into a game where you compete against friends.
 Similar to physical activity in the Strava example, learning is not something that most people enjoy doing. As TikTok founder  Alex Zhu points out, education goes a little against human nature. In combination with a strong enough signaling mechanism however, you can get users to participate. It’s kind of the opposite of  Chris Dixon’s famous “Come for the tool, stay for the network” strategy. Come for the status, stay for the tool.
 A related product I’d love to see is  Strava for Reading. Imagine an eBook reader that not only tracks how much time you spend reading but also *what* you are reading. Based on these proof-of-(reading)-work mechanisms you could build streaks or GitHub-contributions-like visualizations that incentivize users to read more (and more regularly).
  You could even build leaderboards for different topics based on the content of the books and articles you read. Or think about a score that indicated how balanced your reading behavior per topic was (to incentivize users to read takes on political topics from different perspectives).
 Unfortunately, I think it’s unlikely that we will see a product like I described anytime soon.  The world’s largest bookstore, most popular eBook reader, and biggest social network for books are all owned by a company that has very little competency in design and user-facing product innovation.
 (Side note: Amazon’s monopoly on books might be the most underrated sub-optimal equilibrium in tech.)
 Another app that would be interesting is a social investing app. Think “Robinhood but as a social network”. It seems like investing is already quite a social activity – just look at  communities like r/wallstreetbets. As  patio11 pointed out, Robinhood already feels more like a game than a finance app.
  So why not build an investing app that opens with a feed of all your friends’ investments and their returns over time? Instead of sharing screenshots on Reddit and Instagram you could prove your investments right in the app.
 Note that an app like this would not be about signaling wealth. It’s about signaling  being right and the ability to prove it. This is probably an even stronger and more engaging mechanism than signaling wealth – and the reason why I’m still bullish on prediction markets.
 Perhaps a well-designed, consumer-friendly prediction market app would be the ultimate proof-of-x social network.  Strava for being right.
  While we are on the topic of being right: Do you agree with my thoughts in this post? What other social networks and proof-of-x mechanisms would you like to see?
   Thanks to  Dan Romero,   Des Traynor,   Jan König,  Max Cutler and  Zack Hargett for reading drafts of this post.
title: X的证明

note:
Twitter周四宣布，将开始为与联合国安理会各国政府有关联的媒体机构的账户贴上标签。
新的标签不会适用于所有接受政府资助的媒体，根据Twitter宣布这一变化的博客文章，这些媒体只适用于“国家通过财政资源、直接或间接的政治压力和/或对生产和发行的控制来控制编辑内容的媒体”。
Twitter的一位发言人表示，这些标签将出现在中国日报、今日俄罗斯、人造卫星和其他媒体的账户上。但不包括美国之音(Voice Of America)和自由欧洲电台/自由电台(Radio Free Europe/Radio Liberty)，这两家由美国政府资助的媒体机构，也不包括NPR和BBC。这篇博文将NPR和BBC描述为“拥有编辑自主权的国家资助的媒体机构”。
“为了提高透明度和实用性，我们从一组有限且定义明确的国家开始，然后在未来扩大到更广泛的国家，”它说。
推特还将为来自安理会五个常任理事国的一些政府领导人的账户贴上标签，其中包括大使和外长。
此举出台之际，社交网络正因其对外国影响和虚假信息的处理而面临无情的批评。在2016年大选之前，俄罗斯的造谣活动在Twitter和Facebook上猖獗，互联网公司在过去三年里一直在努力防止再次被利用。到目前为止，他们的结果好坏参半；在Facebook上发布关于这种新型冠状病毒的虚假信息的视频已经获得了数千万的点击量，中国政府高层官员在Twitter上推广了关于这种流行病的阴谋论。
但正如Politico详细描述的那样，已经发生了重大变化。Twitter和Facebook已经努力删除关于投票的虚假信息的帖子。上个月，推特(Twitter)撤下了数千个账户，宣扬QAnon阴谋论。
尤其是Twitter，围绕政府官员沟通的任何政策变化都可能面临反弹的风险-特别是来自唐纳德·特朗普(Donald Trump)总统的反弹，他每天都在使用该平台。
周三，该公司在推特上发布了一段总统的视频，称儿童“几乎不受”新冠肺炎的影响，这与特朗普的竞选团队发生了争执。推特锁定了该运动的账户，理由是规则禁止关于大流行的虚假信息。
与此同时，国会共和党人长期以来一直认为，Twitter和其他社交网络选择性地审查保守派观点-这些公司强烈反对这些指控。
note_en:
Twitter will start labeling the accounts of media outlets affiliated with the governments of countries on the U.N. Security Council, it announced Thursday.
 The new labels won’t apply to all media outlets that receive government funding — only “outlets where the state exercises control over editorial content through financial resources, direct or indirect political pressures, and/or control over production and distribution,” according to Twitter’s  blog post announcing the change.
  The labels will go on the accounts for China Daily, Russia Today, Sputnik and other media outlets, a Twitter spokesperson said. But not Voice of America and Radio Free Europe/Radio Liberty, two media outlets funded by the U.S. government, or NPR and the BBC. The blog post described NPR and the BBC as “state-financed media organizations with editorial independence.”
 “For transparency and practicality, we are starting with a limited and clearly-defined group of countries before expanding to a wider range of countries in the future,” it said.
 Twitter will also label the accounts of some government leaders from the five countries that are permanent members of the Security Council, including ambassadors and foreign ministers.
 The move comes as social networks face unstinting criticism for its handling of foreign influence and disinformation. Russian disinformation campaigns ran rampant on Twitter and Facebook in the lead-up to the 2016 election, and internet companies have spent the past three years trying to keep from being used again. Thus far, they’ve had mixed results; videos pushing disinformation about the novel coronavirus have racked up tens of millions of views on Facebook, and high-level Chinese government officials have pushed conspiracy theories about the pandemic on Twitter.
 But there have been significant changes,  as POLITICO has detailed. Twitter and Facebook have worked to take down posts with disinformation about voting. And last month, Twitter yanked thousands of accounts pushing the QAnon conspiracy theory.
 For Twitter in particular, any policy changes around public officials’ communications can risk blowback — especially from President Donald Trump, who uses the platform every day.
 The company jousted with the Trump campaign on Wednesday when it tweeted a video of the president saying children are “almost immune” to Covid-19. Twitter locked the campaign’s account, citing rules against disinformation about the pandemic.
 Congressional Republicans, meanwhile, have long argued that Twitter and other social networks selectively censor conservative views — allegations the companies vehemently dispute.
title: 推特将开始贴上“国家附属媒体”的标签
title_en: Twitter to begin labeling 'state-affiliated media outlets'

新泽西州警察局正在对五人提起网络骚扰指控，这些人与6月份上传到Twitter上的一张抗议照片有关。投诉针对最初的推特用户和其他四个转发了这条消息的人，声称他们让这名警官担心他的家人的安全。
这是反骚扰法史无前例的使用，正值全国执法部门对反警察暴行激进主义的强烈反对。如果成功，这些指控将给社交媒体上的政治活动增加重大的新风险，社交媒体上的政治活动是正在进行的抗议运动中的一个关键因素。
“如果有人知道这个婊子是谁，就把他的信息发布到这条推特下面。”
纳特利警察局在7月下旬对6月26日抗议期间发布的一条推文提出了投诉。这条现已被删除的信息包括一张蒙面值班警察的照片，并要求“如果有人知道这个婊子是谁，就把他的信息放到这条推特上。”由于戴着面具，这名警官很难从照片中辨认出来，而且似乎没有任何回复显示他的身份。
最初的发帖者和转发者被控网络骚扰，这是一项四级重罪，最高可判处18个月监禁。活动人士Gegana Sziszak是转发者之一，他在上周的GoFundMe活动中透露了这一投诉。新泽西州美国公民自由联盟向The Verge证实了它的存在，Sziszak的律师艾伦·佩鲁顿(Alan Peyrouton)也是如此。The Verge还审阅了一份Sziszak的传票副本。
“我从来没有见过这样的事情，”佩鲁顿在谈到这起案件时说。“我看不出这怎么会上升到犯罪的程度。”Sziszak最初要求3000美元聘请律师，到目前为止，她已经筹集了大约8200美元。她的传票命令她在本月晚些时候出庭。
“我看不出这怎么会上升到犯罪的程度。”
该部门代表侦探彼得·桑德梅尼科(Peter Sandomenico)指控Sziszak和其他人，起诉书称他是推文中的警官。它声称，这张照片和附带的说明威胁到这名警官“在履行职责时采取行动，使桑德梅尼科侦探担心自己、家人和财产会受到伤害。”
当Sziszak发布她的筹款活动时，该帖子没有收到回复，并被转发了五次。目前还不清楚该部门是如何发现它的存在的。然而，一些部门使用自动社交媒体监控工具来跟踪从特定位置发送的所有推文。如果这样的工具被用来监视纳特利抗议活动，它很可能已经浮出了桑多梅尼科的推文。
纳特利警察局证实，他们已经对五人提出了网络骚扰投诉。“指控是由我们部门提出的。因为一件与我们的一名警官有关的事件。然而，这件事仍在调查中，这使得我无法提供任何进一步的细节。“安东尼·蒙塔纳里中尉告诉The Verge。该部门还确认了这名警官的身份。
Sziszak在GoFundMe上写道，她被传票弄得措手不及。她写道：“我没有回答，没有说任何针对这名警察的话，对他是谁也一无所知。”“这条推文的目的是找出这名警官的信息，追究他的责任。”
虽然传票中没有提到原始海报的名字，但Sziszak和她的律师证实了他的身份是凯文·阿尔法罗(Kevin Alfaro)，后者在7月份发布了另一个GoFundMe活动。(阿尔法罗没有回复通过Twitter或GoFundMe发送的消息。)。Alfaro的GoFundMe描述了6月26日纳特利争取黑人生活的抗议活动，年轻的反种族主义示威者与亲克里斯托弗·哥伦布的反抗议者对峙。这两个团体最终被路障和警察分开，尽管新泽西州的新闻12报道说没有人被捕。
竞选描述说，阿尔法罗对那些对反抗议者“非常友好”并遮盖徽章的警察感到不安，全国各地的一些警察都采取了这种做法，以躲避抗议者的抱怨。“为了找出哪个警官和骚扰我的人交朋友，我上传了一张照片。”他的推文包括一张印在桑德梅尼科面具上的“细蓝线”美国国旗的照片--这是一个象征警察团结的象征，但充满了种族主义协会。
司法部对这五名推特用户的法律论据含糊其辞。2014年新泽西州的一项法律禁止网络骚扰，当它威胁要对某人进行身体伤害或侵犯他们的财产时，或者当它涉及发送“淫秽、不雅或淫秽”的材料时-这更多地适用于持续的骚扰运动和网络跟踪的案件。第一修正案还保护了对执勤警察拍照的权利。
新泽西州的网络骚扰法关注的是威胁和“淫秽、不雅或淫秽”的内容。
惩罚性转发的先例尤其少，即使在风险较低的非刑事案件中也是如此。2018年，MSNBC主持人乔伊·里德(Joy Reid)因转发他人涉嫌诽谤的帖子而被起诉。但原告很快撤销了转发指控，只关注里德写的声明。最近的一起诉讼称，转发推文可以算作侵犯版权，但尚未解决。抗议转发可能会受到“通信体面法”(Communications Decency Act)第230条的保护，这是一部保护网络服务及其用户免受他人帖子责任的盾牌法律。
起诉书暗示，索要桑德梅尼科的“信息”是对这名警官进行骚扰或安抚的邀请。骚扰受害者起诉了鼓励追随者虐待他们的巨魔-比如蒙大拿州的房地产经纪人坦尼娅·格什(Tanya Gersh)，她去年赢得了针对新纳粹博客作者安德鲁·安格林(Andrew Anglin)的诉讼。但在安格林发表了数十篇关于格什的文章并要求他的追随者制造一场“巨魔风暴”后，格什被吓坏了几个月。纳特利警察局没有报告除了最初的推文之外有任何骚扰，这条推文本身也没有呼吁采取任何行动，只是确认桑德梅尼科的身份。
“这似乎是一名警官在展示他起诉某人的能力。”
美国公民自由联盟-新泽西州最高法院倡导主任亚历山大·沙洛姆(Alexander Shalom)表示，这些指控是似是而非的。“(法律)旨在防止贪腐伤害，而不是过敏症和伤害感情，”他告诉The Verge。“这似乎是一名警官展示了他起诉某人的能力，尽管他无法确定犯罪要素。”
起诉点击转发按钮的人可能会把社交媒体变成雷区。即使只有很小的投诉风险，它也会阻止用户参与内容，特别是那些不确定什么构成骚扰或其他违法行为的用户。而且，对每一个转发了哪怕是一条受欢迎的帖子的人收取费用也几乎是不可能的，这将把它变成一根大棒，让强大的机构-包括警方-可以有选择地用来对付批评者。“如果它被转发了5万次呢？你要去找那5万个转发者吗？“。佩鲁顿问道。“这对我来说是不可思议的。”
无论结果如何，重罪指控都会给被告带来严重的后果。在收到投诉后的几天里，Sziszak被迫管理聘请律师的费用，以及在被冠状病毒大流行颠覆的法律体系中导航的挫折感。即使这些指控不会导致判决，提交的文件也可能会恐吓那些想要合法记录警察的抗议者，或者让其他人不敢与他们接触。
Szisznak写道：“我无法解释我对警察局的恐惧和对我的担忧。”“我担心我可能因为一些我认为正在行使我的第一修正案权利的事情而损害了我的整个未来。”
note_en:
A New Jersey police department is pursuing cyber harassment charges against five people in connection with a protest photo uploaded to Twitter in June. Complaints were served against the original tweeter and four other people who retweeted the message, alleging that they caused the officer to fear for the safety of his family.
 It’s an unprecedented use of anti-harassment laws, coming amid a nationwide law enforcement backlash against anti-police brutality activism. If successful, the charges would add significant new risks to political activity on social media, a key element in the ongoing protest movements.
 “If anyone knows who this bitch is throw his info under this tweet.”
 The Nutley Police Department filed its complaints in late July over a tweet posted during a June 26th protest. The  now-deleted message included a photo of a masked on-duty police officer with a request that “If anyone knows who this bitch is throw his info under this tweet.” Because of the mask, the officer is not readily identifiable from the photograph, and there do not appear to be any replies revealing his identity.
 The original poster and the retweeters are charged with cyber harassment, a fourth-degree felony punishable by up to 18 months in jail. Activist Georgana Sziszak, one of the retweeters, revealed the complaint  in a GoFundMe campaign last week. The American Civil Liberties Union of New Jersey confirmed its existence to  The Verge,  as did Sziszak’s attorney Alan Peyrouton.  The Verge has also reviewed a copy of Sziszak’s summons.
 “I’ve never seen anything like it,” says Peyrouton of the case. “I don’t see how that rises to the level of a crime.” Sziszak originally requested $3,000 to hire an attorney; so far, she’s raised around $8,200. Her summons orders her to appear in court later this month.
 “I don’t see how that rises to the level of a crime.”
 The department charged Sziszak and others on behalf of Detective Peter Sandomenico, who the complaint identifies as the officer in the tweet. It alleges that the photo and accompanying caption threatened the officer “acting in the performance of his duties, causing Detective Sandomenico to fear that harm will come to himself, family, and property.”
 At the time Sziszak posted her fundraiser, the post had no replies and five retweets. It’s unclear how the department discovered its existence. However, some departments use  automated social media surveillance tools to track all the tweets sent from a particular location. Had such a tool been used to surveil the Nutley protests, it would likely have surfaced the Sandomenico tweet.
 The Nutley Police Department confirmed that it had filed complaints against five people for cyber harassment. “Charges were filed by our department. For an incident relating to one of our officers. However this remains under investigation which prevents me from providing any further details,” Detective Lieutenant Anthony Montanari told  The Verge. The department also confirmed the officer’s identity.
 Sziszak wrote on GoFundMe that she had been blindsided by the summons. “I did not reply, did not say anything against this cop, and had zero clue to who he was,” she wrote. “The purpose of this tweet was to find out the officer’s information, to hold him accountable.”
  While the original poster is not named in the summons, Sziszak and her attorney corroborated his identity as Kevin Alfaro, who posted a  separate GoFundMe campaign in July. (Alfaro did not respond to messages via Twitter or GoFundMe.) Alfaro’s GoFundMe describes  a June 26th Nutley For Black Lives protest where young anti-racism demonstrators were  confronted by pro-Christopher Columbus counter-protestors. The groups were eventually separated by barricades and police, although  News 12 New Jersey reported that no arrests were made.
 The campaign description says Alfaro was upset by officers who were “very friendly” with counter-protesters and covered their badges, a practice that  some officers across the country have adopted to dodge complaints from protesters. “In an attempt to identify a specific police officer who was befriending someone harassing me, I uploaded a photo.” His tweet includes a picture of the “Thin Blue Line” American flag printed on Sandomenico’s mask — a symbol that’s used to signify police solidarity but is  fraught with racist associations.
 The department’s legal argument against the five Twitter users is murky. A 2014 New Jersey law bans online harassment when it threatens someone with physical harm or crimes against their property, or when it involves sending “lewd, indecent, or obscene” material — it’s more typically applied in cases involving persistent harassment campaigns and cyberstalking. The First Amendment also  protects the right to photograph on-duty police officers.
 New Jersey’s cyber harassment law focuses on threats and “lewd, indecent, or obscene” material
 There’s particularly little precedent for punishing retweets, even in lower-stakes non-criminal cases. MSNBC host Joy Reid  was sued in 2018 for retweeting someone else’s allegedly defamatory post. But the plaintiff  quickly dropped the retweet accusation, focusing only on statements Reid wrote.  A recent suit claims retweets can count as copyright infringement, but it’s not yet resolved. The protest retweets could potentially be covered by Section 230 of the Communications Decency Act, a shield law that protects web services  and their users from liability over other people’s posts.
 The complaint implies that requesting Sandomenico’s “info” was an invitation to harass or doxx the officer. Harassment victims  have sued trolls who encouraged followers to abuse them — like Montana real estate agent Tanya Gersh, who  won a case against neo-Nazi blogger Andrew Anglin last year. But Gersh was terrorized for months after Anglin published dozens of articles about her and asked his followers to create a “troll storm.” The Nutley Police Department did not report any harassment beyond the original tweet, which itself didn’t call for any action beyond identifying Sandomenico.
 “What this appears to be is a police officer flexing his ability to charge someone.”
 Alexander Shalom, ACLU-NJ’s director of Supreme Court advocacy, declared the charges specious. “[The law] is designed to prevent venal harm, not hypersensitivity and hurt feelings,” he told  The Verge. “What this appears to be is a police officer flexing his ability to charge someone notwithstanding the fact that he cannot establish the elements of the crime.”
 Prosecuting people for clicking the retweet button could turn social media into a minefield. It would discourage users from engaging with content if there’s even a small risk of getting a complaint, especially users who aren’t sure what constitutes harassment or other legal violations. And charging everyone who retweeted even a single popular post would be almost impossible, turning it into a cudgel that powerful institutions — including the police — could selectively use against critics. “What if it had gotten retweeted 50,000 times? Are you going to go out to those 50,000 retweeters?” asks Peyrouton. “It’s inconceivable to me.”
 Regardless of the outcome, the felony charges have serious consequences for the accused. In the days since receiving the complaint, Sziszak has been forced to manage the expense of hiring an attorney and the frustration of navigating a legal system upended by the coronavirus pandemic. Even if the charges don’t result in a sentence, the filing could intimidate protesters who want to legally record police, or make others afraid to engage with them.
 “I cannot explain the fear I have and the worry I have for me vs. a police department,” Szisznak writes. “I feel afraid that I may have compromised my whole future based on something that I had believed was exercising my First Amendment right.”
title: 新泽西州警方因转发一名警官的照片而受到重罪指控
title_en: New Jersey police are pressing felony charges for retweeting an officer’s photo

在对唐纳德·特朗普(Donald Trump)煽动性或误导性帖子的处理方式进行了数月的辩论和分歧后，Facebook员工希望首席执行官马克·扎克伯格(Mark Zuckerberg)解释说，如果这位自由世界的领导人利用这个社交网络破坏2020年美国总统大选的结果，公司会怎么做。
Facebook的一名员工本周早些时候在该公司内部沟通平台Workplace的一个群组中写道：“我确实认为，我们正走向一个有问题的场景，Facebook将被用来以历史上从未有过的方式，积极破坏美国选举的合法性。”
在过去的一周里，这种情况一直是Facebook内部激烈讨论的话题，现在也是其领导人的首要问题。约2900名员工要求扎克伯格在周四上午11点举行的全公司会议上公开发表讲话。PT.。
扎克伯格意料之中的讲话正值内部对该公司处理错误信息的能力日益担忧之际，以及该公司为确保其平台不会在美国总统大选前被用来扰乱或误导而采取的预防措施。尽管Facebook表示，它已经投入了更多的资金和资源，以避免在2016年大选期间重蹈覆辙，但一些员工认为这还不够。特朗普总统已经花了几个月的时间质疑即将到来的2020年大选的合法性，散布关于邮寄选票的错误信息，并拒绝透露他是否接受在11月输给民主党候选人乔·拜登的可能性。
今年7月，特朗普告诉福克斯新闻(Fox News)，他不确定自己是否会向拜登让步，这让人怀疑，如果这位前副总统赢得选举，权力是否会和平过渡。我得看看。我不会简单地说“是”。我不会说不，总统说。
在Facebook的内部留言板上，在周四的全体会议之前，关于特朗普选举问题的讨论仍然是文明的。员工们就审查在任总统关于选举结果的潜在虚假陈述的好处展开了辩论，其中一人指出，“这将是一项真正令人不安的政策，适用于全球。”
美国不能让Facebook在谈到我们民主的完整性时采取观望态度，“希拉里·克林顿(Hillary Clinton)的前外交政策发言人、非营利性倡导组织Responsible Tech的联合创始人杰西·莱里奇(Jesse Lehrich)说。“除非他们主动勾勒出明确的政策和执行机制来保障选举，否则政纲将被武器化，以破坏它。
你在Facebook或其他科技公司工作吗？我们很高兴收到你的来信。联系Craig.silverman@buzzfeed.com，ryan.mac@buzzfeed.com，或通过我们的举报热线渠道之一。
虽然有迹象表明，在特朗普违反规定的情况下，脸书将挺身而出--比如周三，脸书删除了总统发布的一段视频，在视频中，特朗普声称儿童“几乎不受”新冠肺炎的影响--但也有其他人表示，该公司正在屈服于右翼的批评声音。在Workplace最近的另一篇帖子中，一名高级工程师收集的内部证据显示，Facebook正在优待知名的保守派账户，以帮助他们从内容中删除事实核查。
作为回应，该公司取消了他的职位，并限制内部访问他引用的信息。根据BuzzFeed新闻看到的内部帖子，这名工程师周三被解雇。
随着内部紧张局势的加剧和士气处于低谷，一个致力于错误信息政策的内部Workplace小组对该公司如何处理事实核查内容的担忧激增。
上周五，在另一次全体会议上，员工们问扎克伯格，右翼出版物布莱巴特新闻(Breitbart News)在分享了一段宣传未经证实的治疗方法的视频后，如何才能继续成为Facebook新闻的合作伙伴，并表示口罩对于抗击这种新型冠状病毒是不必要的。这段视频在被从布莱巴特的页面上删除之前，在6个小时内就获得了1400万的点击量，尽管其他账号仍在分享它。
扎克伯格回避了这个问题，但确实指出，如果布莱巴特在90天内因发布错误信息而受到两次罢工，它可能会被从公司的新闻标签中删除。(Facebook新闻合作伙伴，包括BuzzFeed News和华盛顿邮报(The Washington Post)等数十种出版物，可以获得补偿，并在社交网络上的一个特别新闻选项卡中获得安置。)。
扎克伯格说：“这当然是他们因为错误信息而遭受的一次打击，但在过去的90天里，他们没有其他的打击。”“因此，根据我们现有的政策(顺便说一句，我认为这些政策在这方面总体上是相当合理的)，取消这些政策是没有意义的。”
但Facebook自己的一些员工收集的证据显示，布莱巴特-以及其他右翼媒体和人物，包括Turning Point USA创始人查理·柯克(Charlie Kirk)、特朗普的支持者钻石和希尔克(Diamond And Silk)，以及保守的视频制作非营利性组织普拉格大学(Prager University)-受到了特殊对待，帮助它避免了与公司政策的冲突。他们认为这是对右翼出版商和页面的优待模式的一部分，其中许多人声称社交网络对保守派有偏见。
Facebook发言人利兹·布尔乔瓦在一份声明中说：“我们尊重第三方事实核查人员对一条内容的评级。”当事实检查员应用评级时，我们会应用标签和降级。但我们有责任为惯犯管理我们的内部系统。我们对多个虚假评级适用额外的系统范围处罚，包括去货币化和无法做广告，除非我们确定这些评级中的一个或多个不值得承担额外的后果。&#34；
7月22日，Facebook的一名员工向公司内部错误信息政策小组发布了一条消息，指出Facebook似乎代表该出版物行事的人已经澄清了针对布莱巴特的一些错误信息攻击。
这位员工写道：“布莱巴特升级标记为‘紧急：当天结束’的事件在同一天得到解决，所有针对布莱巴特页面和他们域名的错误信息罢工都在没有任何解释的情况下被清除。”
这名员工表示，该公司负责全球公共政策的副总裁乔尔·卡普兰(Joel Kaplan)表示，对查理·柯克(Charlie Kirk)在Instagram上发布的一篇帖子的部分错误评级被标记为“优先”升级。卡普兰曾在乔治·W·布什(George W.Bush)政府任职，并因公开支持布雷特·卡瓦诺(Brett Kavanaugh)有争议的最高法院提名而招致批评。
PolitiFact的执行董事亚伦·沙洛克曼(Aaron Sharockman)告诉BuzzFeed新闻，Facebook的一名联系人确实打电话讨论了柯克的帖子。
沙洛克曼说：“我们和他们通了电话，他们想知道这个帖子是如何与这个项目相一致的。”这只是一个微小的不准确，还是我们认为有潜在有害影响的东西？
PolitiFact没有改变对这篇帖子的评级。“我们坚持己见，”沙洛克曼说。
Facebook过去的员工，包括Facebook前全球选举广告诚信主管雅尔·艾森斯塔特(Yaël Eisenstat)，都对卡普兰对内容执法决定的影响力表示担忧。她之前告诉BuzzFeed新闻，卡普兰华盛顿政策团队的一名成员试图影响一个保守组织发布的广告的广告执行决定。
Facebook没有回应为什么卡普兰会亲自干预这样的事情的问题。
这些干预和其他干预似乎违反了Facebook的官方政策，该政策要求希望对事实核查评级提出异议的出版商与Facebook负责的事实核查合作伙伴联系。
另一名员工在该公司内部的“错误信息政策”讨论组中写道：“政策人员似乎一直在代表*完全*右翼出版商干预事实核查，以避免他们再次犯规。”
公开表示对右翼页面明显特殊对待的个人也面临后果。在一个案例中，Facebook的一名高级工程师收集了多个保守派人物从Facebook员工(包括政策团队中的员工)那里获得独特帮助的例子，以消除对其内容的事实核查。他7月份的帖子被删除了，因为这违反了公司的“尊重沟通政策”。
根据Workplace另一名员工的帖子，在这名工程师的职位被解除后，他引用的相关内部“任务”作为所谓的特殊待遇的例子，被设置为私人的，员工无法访问。
“就我个人而言，这让我对这家公司感到愤怒和羞愧，”这名员工写道，以支持他们的同事。
这位工程师于2016年加入公司，最近在Instagram上工作。他于周三离开了公司。BuzzFeed新闻看到的一名内部帖子上的一名员工说，他们得到了工程师的许可，可以说解雇“不是自愿的”。
在被解雇前的一篇内部帖子中，这位工程师表示，他“被告知，如果我的帖子被发现违反了尊重员工之外的其他政策，法律和人力资源部将与他联系。”
Facebook否认这名员工已经被解雇，但表示这是因为他们违反了公司的规定。
“我们有一种开放的文化，鼓励员工畅所欲言，”布尔乔瓦说。
他被解雇的消息导致Facebook的一些员工表示，他们现在害怕在内部讨论中批评公司。一人表示，他们正在删除旧帖子和评论，而另一人则表示，这“并不是第一次用尊重的工作场所指导方针来狙击一位著名的公司政策/道德批评者。”
另一名员工表示：“(他)是这家公司的良知，是我们做正确事情的不知疲倦的代言人。”
这名被解雇的员工拒绝置评，并要求不具名，因为担心引起反响。
2019年9月，Facebook创始人兼首席执行官马克·扎克伯格和Facebook全球公共政策副总裁乔尔·卡普兰在离开国会山与参议员约翰·科宁的会议后聊天。
这位工程师收集的内部证据与一名为Facebook的一家美国事实核查合作伙伴工作的记者的经验一致。他们告诉BuzzFeed新闻，保守的页面经常直接向公司抱怨。
“不按程序办事的出版商，似乎多是右翼的。他们没有向事实核查人员提出上诉，而是立即打电话给他们在Facebook的代表，“这位记者说。由于他们没有被授权公开发言，他拒绝透露姓名。“他们直接跳起来，说‘审查制度，第一修正案，自由。’”
他们补充说：“我认为Facebook有点害怕他们，因为特朗普政府。”
Facebook通常会将专门的合作伙伴经理指派到追随者较多或广告预算较大的页面。他们帮助客户最大限度地利用该平台。但在工程师的帖子中确定的案例中，合作伙伴代表似乎寻求对右翼出版商的优惠待遇。这导致了Facebook人员给事实核查合作伙伴打电话，以及在事实核查人员不知情或没有参与的情况下，错误信息罢工似乎已从内容中删除的情况。
Facebook的一名员工在7月22日的帖子中重申了这位工程师的发现，发现了多起来自右翼页面的事实核查投诉升级的情况，在某些情况下，投诉在同一天得到了有利于该账户的解决。
根据这篇帖子，乔尔·卡普兰(Joel Kaplan)在Instagram上对查理·柯克(Charlie Kirk)的一篇帖子进行了事实核查，要求“尽快/在下午12点之前”解决。ET“。这名员工声称，PragerU的合作伙伴经理是一项“为期两周的努力”的一部分，目的是防止该网站被授予惯犯身份，而这一称号将限制其覆盖范围和广告特权。
该代表以“合作伙伴敏感性”为例指出，PragerU投放了大量广告，并辩称相关内容符合观点，因此不受事实核查。
Facebook没有回答为什么合作伙伴经理会以广告量为理由不对一组页面采取行动。
沙洛克曼说，PolitiFact在Facebook的联系人从未要求他们改变评级。但Facebook的代表确实会主动讨论一篇帖子是否属于观点，是否超出了该计划的范围。
“我们进行了讨论，我们的合作伙伴问我们，‘你们为什么要对此进行事实核查？你为什么会得出这个结论呢？你认为这是否符合Facebook在事实核查方面的规章制度？他说。
布尔乔瓦说：我们有时会联系事实核查人员，以澄清我们的指导方针和该计划的范围。
马克·普罗沃斯特管理着多个不断进步的大型Facebook页面，包括其他98%的页面，这是该平台上最大的页面之一。他说，他的Facebook合作伙伴经理的反应远远低于Facebook员工为右边的Provost同行记录的内容。他也不知道Facebook曾代表他联系过任何事实核查人员。
普罗沃斯特在谈到脸书时说：“我们10天内都不会收到回信。”“我想右翼得到了一笔更好的交易。”
他的另一个98%的页面目前有被删除的风险，因为它有三次罢工。教务长说，其中一次罢工是因为三个月前违反了仇恨言论，尽管到目前为止，Facebook还没有人告诉他这篇冒犯的帖子是什么。据教务长称，他的合作伙伴代表表示，他们将对此进行调查。那是上周的事了，他说他还没有收到最新消息。
“我对此感到非常沮丧，”教务长说。“最好的解决方案是，如果Facebook像他们指派的事实核查公司一样做出反应。”
普罗沃斯特说，当他联系Facebook的事实核查伙伴对评级提出异议时，很容易与他们打交道。在某些情况下，他提供了额外的证据，证明他声称自己改变了评级；在另一些情况下，他纠正了违规帖子，错误的评级被删除。
接受BuzzFeed新闻采访的事实核查人员表示，大多数页面所有者都遵循这项政策，并与他们联系，对评级提出异议。但在某些情况下，他们会直接听取被指派与事实核查人员合作的Facebook合作伙伴经理的意见。
“他们会问我们，‘你能再看看这个吗？你确定吗？‘“。收银员说。
在其他情况下，Facebook本身将悄悄地删除其合作伙伴之一应用的事实核查。这似乎就是3月25日钻石与丝绸(Diamond And Silk)的一篇帖子所发生的事情。两人在Facebook上写道：“该死的，为了给众议院议员加薪，怎么会拨出2500万美元，一点也不关心美国人，这将有助于刺激美国的经济？”告诉我怎么做？#PutamericansBackToWorkNow。“
Facebook的事实核查合作伙伴Lead Stories将这篇帖子评为虚假。戴蒙德和西尔克最初遵循了适当的程序，直接呼吁进行头条新闻。
《头条》主编艾伦·杜克(Alan Duke)告诉BuzzFeed新闻：“他们的呼吁很详细，我们很快就做出了回应。”“结果是，我们决定，最初给出内容的虚假评级应该向下修正为‘部分虚假’。”
但这一部分虚假的评级显然并不能让戴蒙德和西尔克满意，据报道，他们在传播冠状病毒错误信息后失去了在线福克斯新闻节目。正如7月22日Facebook内部帖子所述，戴蒙德和西尔克呼吁他们的合作伙伴经理，后者为这一问题开了一张内部罚单。Facebook的代表辩称，这篇帖子只是观点，并警告说，这两人“非常敏感，毫不犹豫地公开了他们对Facebook上所谓的保守派偏见的担忧。”
这位Facebook员工说，“有人在任务中被描述为‘政策/领导层’，他呼吁不仅要完全取消这次罢工，还要取消从1月份开始的罢工。”
note_en:
After months of debate and disagreement over the handling of inflammatory or misleading posts from Donald Trump, Facebook employees want CEO Mark Zuckerberg to explain what the company would do if the leader of the free world uses the social network to undermine the results of the 2020 US presidential election.
 “I do think we’re headed for a problematic scenario where Facebook is going to be used to aggressively undermine the legitimacy of the US elections, in a way that has never been possible in history,” one Facebook employee wrote in a group on Workplace, the company’s internal communication platform, earlier this week.
 For the past week, this scenario has been a topic of heated discussion inside Facebook and is now a top question for its leader. Some 2,900 employees asked that Zuckerberg address it publicly during a company-wide meeting on Thursday which is slated for 11 a.m. PT.
 Zuckerberg’s expected remarks come amid  growing internal concerns about the company's competence in handling misinformation, and the precautions it is taking to ensure its platform isn’t used to disrupt or mislead ahead of the US presidential election. Though Facebook says it has committed more money and resources to avoid repeating its failures during the 2016 election, some employees believe it isn’t enough. President Trump has already spent months raising questions about the legitimacy of the upcoming 2020 election,  spreading misinformation about mail-in ballots, and declining to say if he’d accept the possibility of losing to Democratic nominee Joe Biden in November.
 In July,  Trump told Fox News he wasn’t sure if he’d concede to Biden, casting doubt on whether there would be a peaceful transition of power if the former vice president wins the election. "I have to see. I'm not just going to say yes. I'm not going to say no," the president said.
    On Facebook’s internal message boards, discussion about the Trump election question remained civil prior to Thursday’s all-hands meeting. Employees debated the merits of censoring a sitting president’s potentially false statements about election results with one person noting that “it would be a really troubling policy to apply globally.”
 "America can't afford for Facebook to take a wait-and-see approach when it comes to the integrity of our democracy,” said Jesse Lehrich, a former foreign policy spokesperson to Hillary Clinton and cofounder of Accountable Tech, a nonprofit advocacy group. “Unless they proactively outline clear policies and enforcement mechanisms to safeguard the election, the platform  will be weaponized to undermine it."
 Do you work at Facebook or another technology company? We'd love to hear from you. Reach out at  craig.silverman@buzzfeed.com,   ryan.mac@buzzfeed.com,  or  via one of our   tip line channels .
 While there are signs Facebook will stand up to Trump in cases where he violates its rules — as on Wednesday when  it removed a video post from the president in which he claimed that children are “almost immune” to  COVID-19 — there are others who suggest the company is caving to critical voices on the right. In another recent Workplace post, a senior engineer collected internal evidence that showed Facebook was giving preferential treatment to prominent conservative accounts to help them remove fact-checks from their content.
 The company responded by removing his post and restricting internal access to the information he cited. On Wednesday the engineer was fired, according to internal posts seen by BuzzFeed News.
  With  heightened internal tensions and morale at a low point, concerns about how the company handles fact-checked content have exploded in an internal Workplace group dedicated to misinformation policy.
 Last Friday, at another all-hands meeting, employees asked Zuckerberg how right-wing publication Breitbart News could remain a Facebook News partner after sharing a video that promoted unproven treatments and said masks were unnecessary to combat the novel coronavirus. The video racked up 14 million views in six hours  before it was removed from Breitbart’s page, though other accounts continued to share it.
 Zuckerberg danced around the question but did note that Breitbart could be removed from the company's news tab if it were to receive two strikes for publishing misinformation within 90 days of each other. (Facebook News partners, which include dozens of publications such as BuzzFeed News and the Washington Post, receive compensation and placement in a special news tab on the social network.)
 “This was certainly one strike against them for misinformation, but they don't have others in the last 90 days,” Zuckerberg said. “So by the policies that we have, which by the way I think are generally pretty reasonable on this, it doesn't make sense to remove them.”
 But some of Facebook’s own employees gathered evidence they say shows Breitbart — along with other right-wing outlets and figures including Turning Point USA founder Charlie Kirk, Trump supporters Diamond and Silk, and conservative video production nonprofit  Prager University — has received special treatment that helped it avoid running afoul of company policy. They see it as part of a pattern of preferential treatment for right-wing publishers and pages, many of which have alleged that the social network is biased against conservatives.
 “We defer to third-party fact-checkers on the rating that a piece of content receives," Facebook spokesperson Liz Bourgeois said in a statement. "When a fact checker applies a rating, we apply a label and demotion. But we are responsible for how we manage our internal systems for repeat offenders. We apply additional system wide penalties for multiple false ratings, including demonetization and the inability to advertise, unless we determine that one or more of those ratings does not warrant additional consequences."
 On July 22, a Facebook employee posted a message to the company’s internal misinformation policy group noting that some misinformation strikes against Breitbart had been cleared by someone at Facebook seemingly acting on the publication's behalf.
 “A Breitbart escalation marked ‘urgent: end of day’ was resolved on the same day, with all misinformation strikes against Breitbart’s page and against their domain cleared without explanation,” the employee wrote.
 The same employee said a partly false rating applied to an Instagram post from Charlie Kirk was flagged for “priority” escalation by Joel Kaplan, the company’s vice president of global public policy. Kaplan once served in George W. Bush’s administration and drew criticism for publicly supporting Brett Kavanaugh’s controversial nomination to the Supreme Court.
 Aaron Sharockman, the executive director of PolitiFact, told BuzzFeed News a contact at Facebook did call to discuss Kirk’s post.
 “We had a call with them where they wanted to know how this post was aligned with the program,” Sharockman said. "Was this just a minor inaccuracy or was it something that we thought was something that had potential harmful effects?"
 PolitiFact did not change its rating on the post. “We stuck to our guns there,” Sharockman said.
 Past Facebook employees, including Yaël Eisenstat, Facebook's former global election ads integrity lead, have expressed concerns with Kaplan’s influence over content enforcement decisions. She  previously told BuzzFeed News a member of Kaplan's Washington policy team attempted to influence ad enforcement decisions for an ad placed by a conservative organization.
 Facebook did not respond to questions about why Kaplan would personally intervene in matters like this.
    These and other interventions appear to be in violation of Facebook’s  official policy, which requires publishers wishing to dispute a fact check rating to contact the Facebook fact-checking partner responsible.
 “It appears that policy people have been intervening in fact-checks on behalf of *exclusively* right-wing publishers, to avoid them getting repeat-offender status,” wrote another employee in the company’s internal “misinformation policy” discussion group.
 Individuals that spoke out about the apparent special treatment of right-wing pages have also faced consequences. In one case, a senior Facebook engineer collected multiple instances of conservative figures receiving unique help from Facebook employees, including those on the policy team, to remove fact-checks on their content. His July post was removed because it violated the company’s “respectful communication policy.”
 After the engineer’s post was removed, the related internal “tasks” he’d cited as examples of the alleged special treatment were made private and inaccessible to employees, according to a Workplace post from another employee.
 “Personally this makes me so angry and ashamed of this company,” wrote the employee in support of their colleague.
 The engineer joined the company in 2016 and most recently worked on Instagram. He left the company on Wednesday. One employee on an internal thread seen by BuzzFeed News said that they received permission from the engineer to say that the dismissal “was not voluntary.”
 In an internal post before his dismissal, the engineer said he was “told to expect to be contacted by legal and HR if my post is found to be violating other policies in addition to Respectful Comms.”
 Facebook denied the employee had been terminated for the post but said it was because "they broke the company's rules."
 “We have an open culture and encourage employees to speak out about concerns they have," Bourgeois said.
 News of his firing caused some Facebook employees to say that they now fear speaking critically about the company in internal discussions. One person said they were deleting old posts and comments, while another said this was “hardly the first time the respectful workplace guidelines have been used to snipe a prominent critic of company policies/ethics.”
 “[He] was a conscience of this company, and a tireless voice for us doing the right thing,” said another employee.
 The terminated employee declined to comment and asked not to be named for fear of repercussions.
  Facebook founder and CEO Mark Zuckerberg and Facebook's Vice President of Global Public Policy Joel Kaplan chat after leaving a meeting with Sen. John Cornyn on Capitol Hill in September 2019.
  The internal evidence gathered by the engineer aligns with the experience of a journalist who works for one of Facebook’s US fact-checking partners. They told BuzzFeed News that conservative pages often complain directly to the company.
 “Of the publishers that don’t follow the procedure, it seems to be mostly ones on the right. Instead of appealing to the fact-checker they immediately call their rep at Facebook,” said the journalist, who declined to be named because they were not authorized to speak publicly. “They jump straight up and say ‘censorship, First Amendment, freedom.’”
 “I think Facebook is a bit afraid of them because of the Trump administration,” they added.
    Facebook typically assigns dedicated partner managers to pages with large followings or big ad budgets. They help their clients maximize their use of the platform. But in the cases identified in the engineer's post, partner reps appear to have sought preferential treatment for right-wing publishers. This resulted in phone calls to fact-checking partners from people at Facebook, and instances where misinformation strikes appear to have been removed from content without a fact-checker’s knowledge or involvement.
 A Facebook employee’s July 22 post restating the engineer’s findings identified multiple cases in which a fact-check complaint from a right-wing page was escalated and in some cases resolved in the account’s favor the same day.
 According to the post, Joel Kaplan flagged a fact check of a Charlie Kirk Instagram post for resolution “ASAP/before 12 p.m. ET”. This same employee claimed PragerU's partner manager was part of a “two weeks long effort” to prevent the site from being given Repeat Offender status, a designation that would have limited its reach and advertising privileges.
 Citing “partner sensitivity," the rep noted that PragerU runs a lot of ads, and argued that the content in question qualified as opinion and was therefore exempt from being fact-checked.
 Facebook did not answer questions about why a partner manager would cite ad volume as a reason for not acting against a group of pages.
 Sharockman said PolitiFact’s contacts at Facebook have never asked them to change a rating. But Facebook reps do reach out to discuss whether a post is opinion or otherwise outside of the scope of the program.
 “We have had discussions where our partners have asked us, ‘Why did you fact check this? Why did you come to this conclusion? How do you think it fits within the scope of Facebook's rules or regulations within fact-checking?’” he said.
 "We sometimes reach out to fact-checkers to clarify our guidelines and scope of the program," Bourgeois said.
 Mark Provost manages multiple large progressive Facebook pages, including The Other 98%, one of the biggest on the platform. He said his Facebook partner manager is far less responsive than what the Facebook employee documented for Provost’s counterparts on the right. And he’s not aware of any case where Facebook contacted a fact-checker on his behalf.
 “We don't get a message back for 10 days,” Provost said of Facebook. “I imagine the right wing is getting a way better deal.”
 His Other 98% page is currently at risk of deletion because it has three strikes. Provost said one strike is for a hate speech violation three months ago, though as of now no one at Facebook has told him what the offending post was. His partner representative said they would look into it, according to Provost. That was last week and he says he still hasn’t received an update.
 “I'm getting so frustrated with this,” Provost said. “The best solution would be if Facebook were as responsive as the fact-checking companies that they've assigned.”
 Provost said Facebook’s fact-checking partners are easy to deal with when he contacts them to dispute a rating. In some cases, he provided additional proof of his claim to get a rating changed; in other cases, he corrected the offending post and the false rating was removed.
 The fact-checker who spoke to BuzzFeed News said most page owners follow the policy and contact them to dispute a rating. But in some cases, they hear directly from the Facebook partner manager assigned to work with fact-checkers.
 “They will ask us, ‘Could you take a look at this again? Are you sure?’” the checker said.
 In other cases, Facebook itself will quietly remove a fact-check applied by one of its partners. That appears to be what happened with a March 25 post from Diamond and Silk. The duo  wrote on Facebook, “How the hell is allocating 25 million dollars in order to give a raise to house members, that don't give a damn about Americans, going to help stimulate America's economy? Tell me how? #PutAmericansBackToWorkNow.”
 Lead Stories, a Facebook fact-checking partner, rated the post false. Diamond and Silk initially followed the proper procedure and appealed directly to Lead Stories.
 “They were detailed in their appeal and we replied promptly,” Alan Duke, the editor-in-chief of Lead Stories, told BuzzFeed News. “The result was that we decided that a false rating initially given their content should be revised downward to ‘partly false.’”
 But that partly false rating apparently didn’t satisfy Diamond and Silk, who  reportedly lost their online Fox News show after spreading coronavirus misinformation. As detailed in the July 22 internal Facebook post, Diamond and Silk appealed to their partner manager, who opened an internal ticket for the issue. The Facebook rep argued the post was opinion and warned that the duo “is extremely sensitive and has not hesitated going public about their concerns around alleged conservative bias on Facebook.”
 The Facebook employee said that “someone described on the task as ‘Policy/Leadership’ made the call to not only completely remove this strike, but also the one from January.” ●
